scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: Every key file is named with concrete paths and line-level citations (chat_completion.rb:73, :297, :340; function_dispatch.rb:46, :126; function_tool_adapter.rb:50; mcp/tool.rb, sse_client.rb, stdio_client.rb), and the answer traces the end-to-end pipeline from entry point through dispatch to schema building; an agent could pick any entry point and proceed with only targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every abstraction is tied to a named module, file, and line number rather than vague descriptions; 'ChatCompletion#chat_completion (chat_completion.rb:73)', 'FunctionDispatch.function (function_dispatch.rb:46)', 'FunctionToolAdapter (function_tool_adapter.rb:50)' are all actionable by an agent.

justification.rationale: The answer labels modules as 'mixins' and 'Rails concern' and implies the a-la-carte pattern ('hosts use to declare callable functions', 'registers each remote tool through the same FunctionDispatch path') but never articulates WHY the composable mixin design was chosen over a monolithic class; the design rationale is absent.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere: the answer presents the composition model and all line numbers as settled fact, leaving the agent without any signal to verify edge cases like how PromptDeclarations overrides ChatCompletion or how the loop re-enters after tool results.

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces all ten layers of the round-trip with file:line throughout — entry at chat_completion.rb:73, FunctionDispatch override, tools param build at :91-97, message adaptation, model send at :147 with FunctionToolAdapter at :382-385, tool-call detection at :160-161, max-calls guard at :165-177, dispatch loop at :180-187, atomic transcript append at function_dispatch.rb:80-101, recursion at :194-209, and termination at :221-235. An agent could open any layer directly; only small targeted lookups (e.g., the filtered_tools path, the exact response-normalisation shape at :424-439) remain.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named exactly — ChatCompletion#chat_completion, FunctionDispatch, FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm, MessageAdapters::Base, max_tool_calls, stop_tool_calls_and_respond — all with file:line; nothing is left as 'the chat loop' or 'the dispatch code'.

justification.rationale: The answer explains the mechanism behind auto-continuation ('unless stop_tool_calls_and_respond! was called, recurses … with decremented max_tool_calls') and how results re-enter ('appends … assistant/tool_calls message AND the tool result message … rebuilds updated_messages = transcript.flatten.compact'), but frames these as what-happens rather than why the design chose recursion + atomic append; the design motivation is left implicit.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer names all three branching conditions (max_tool_calls cutoff at :165-177, stop_tool_calls_and_respond at :210-218, loop guard at :198) and the FunctionDispatch super-call ordering, but presents them as established facts rather than flagging them as things the agent should verify before acting; there is no explicit 'confirm this branch before changing it' signal.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full declaration-to-schema path with file:line at every hop: DSL at function_dispatch.rb:46, @functions storage at :47/:48-65, tools list at :126-130, FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm at function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57, wiring into chat_completion.rb:91-97, and both filtering paths (filtered_tools :303-313 and MCP only:/except: mcp.rb:85-93). An agent could add a new declared capability with only small lookups to confirm exact parameter-schema syntax.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named exactly — FunctionDispatch.function, @functions, attr_reader :functions, tools, FunctionToolAdapter, create_tool_from_function, filtered_tools, UndeclaredToolError — each tied to a concrete file:line rather than 'the function code'. The false/nil semantics for the available_tools: arg are also spelled out.

justification.rationale: The answer names and locates each method but does not explain WHY the design is a class-level DSL included into a host class (so tools are declared declaratively per host, not hardcoded centrally). The anonymous RubyLLM::Tool subclass and execute re-delegation give implicit WHY for the adapter layer, but the composable-mixin motivation is absent.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer notes additionalProperties:false and strict:true defaults and the false/nil/name filtering semantics, which serve as implicit cautions, but never explicitly flags where an agent is likely to get things wrong (e.g., parameter schema shape, required-vs-optional handling at :54-63, naming collisions). No 'verify before acting' language anywhere.

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three adapter layers are named with file:line (transcript_adapter.rb:5, message_adapters/base.rb:17-27, multimodal_content_adapter.rb:20-38) and sequenced in flow order with invocation sites at chat_completion.rb:133,139,349-372; an agent could open any of these files and start editing without further exploration.

specificity.rationale: Concrete line ranges are given for every method discussed (e.g., add_message_from_hash :63-80, normalize_message_format :99-119, translate :20-38, image_url base64 :51-61) rather than vague module-level pointers, leaving almost no ambiguity about where to find each transform.

justification.rationale: The answer explains each adapter's function ('converts OpenAI image_url arrays into RubyLLM::Content', 'abbreviated vs full formats') but never states the overarching reason: that the pipeline normalizes diverse conversation shapes into the provider-specific wire format; the why is implicit in the mechanics, not articulated.

uncertainty.rationale: Conditional paths (Anthropic claude-3 cache-control at :43-45, base64 data: URIs at :51-61) are described factually but not flagged as 'verify before acting'; the agent is given no explicit signal about where the adaptation is conditional on provider or content type.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: The answer maps every piece the rubric names with file:line: Raix::MCP::Tool at mcp/tool.rb:11, both transport clients at sse_client.rb:32/47/86 and stdio_client.rb:16/26/59, and the joining mechanism at mcp.rb:70-162 including the include FunctionDispatch, the function() registration, the @functions overwrite, and the chat_completion.rb:184/297 dispatch side. An agent could open any file and start editing immediately.

specificity.rationale: Every file is named by its exact path (mcp/tool.rb, mcp/sse_client.rb, mcp/stdio_client.rb, mcp.rb) with tight line spans; key symbols like Tool.from_json, call_tool, coerce_arguments, and unique_key are anchored to concrete lines rather than described vaguely.

justification.rationale: The answer explains WHY the paths converge: MCP tools land in the same @functions array via function() + schema overwrite, so dispatch_tool_function and the authorization check treat them identically; the closing paragraph ties the mechanism to the effect explicitly, not just listing steps.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the MCP path with full confidence and never flags where it differs from local functions in ways that could trip an agent (e.g., argument coercion semantics, error propagation from client.call_tool, or whether all transcript fields are truly identical); the agent is given no explicit cues about what to verify before assuming parity.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: All rubric-mandated areas are present with file:line: chat_completion.rb dispatch loop (`:160-187`, `:297-299`, `:382-385`), function_dispatch.rb DSL (`:46-107`), function_tool_adapter.rb schema conversion (`:6-57`), all three message adapters, the full MCP surface (mcp.rb, tool.rb, sse_client.rb, stdio_client.rb), and a wide spec matrix. An agent could open each area and know exactly what to touch; the only thing that might warrant one extra lookup is where the host class `include`s these mixins.

specificity.rationale: Every entry is a concrete file:line or file:line-range (e.g. `chat_completion.rb:160-187`, `function_tool_adapter.rb:6-48`, `spec/raix/mcp_spec.rb:330-390`); there is no vague 'the function code' language anywhere in the answer.

justification.rationale: Section headers (Chat loop / dispatch, Declaration & schema conversion, External tools, Tests) implicitly encode WHY each group is affected, but the answer never explicitly states the causal chain: e.g. that function_tool_adapter.rb is affected because it owns schema format the model reads, or that the adapters are affected because they shape the tool-result message re-entering the loop. The WHY is inferrable but not stated.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no uncertainty at all: it does not note that mixin activation depends on host-class includes (invisible to static analysis), that MCP transport call-paths (sse vs stdio) need live verification, or that test fixtures may encode the wire schema and need updating; an agent acting on this list could miss those blind spots.

map_quality.rationale: All five areas are covered with concrete file:line throughout — declaration (function_dispatch.rb:46-65, :67-106, :126-130), schema conversion (function_tool_adapter.rb:6-48, :50-57), chat loop (chat_completion.rb:91-97, :160-187, :194-218, etc.), adapters (transcript_adapter.rb:63-119, base.rb:17-47, multimodal:20-61), and external path (mcp.rb:99-158, mcp/tool.rb, clients); specs to update and add are named with lines. A teammate could open the PR from this map alone.

specificity.rationale: Every change point carries a file:line (e.g. 'chat_completion.rb:160-187(:184,:186)', 'mcp.rb:108-117 registration, :120-157 dispatch proxy, :131-152 transcript mirror') rather than vague area names; even the new specs are scoped to a real fixture file (spec/support/mcp_server.rb).

justification.rationale: Most entries explain the pipeline role — e.g. function_tool_adapter.rb 'must mirror any new declaration fields, or they won't reach the model' and mcp.rb ':131-152 must stay in lockstep with FunctionDispatch's shape'; the adapter section uses conditional 'if' clauses that name the triggering change, which is sufficient though not deep.

uncertainty.rationale: Conditional 'if the feed-back contract changes' / 'if a new message/content type is introduced' language implies scope uncertainty, and 'real server, no mocking per mcp.rb:69' is one verification note; but the map never explicitly flags where the agent should verify before committing (loop re-entry mechanics, exact schema format expected by ruby_llm, MCP transport call contract, fixture coverage) leaving those as silent assumptions.
